title: Colonisation of the Cape in the 17th and 18th Centuries

sources.title: Grade 7 - Term 3: Colonisation of the Cape in the 17th and 18th centuries
Dutch settlement, the Indian Ocean slave trade and slavery at the Cape - seventeenth and eighteenth centuries
History of slavery and early colonisation in South Africa
Cape Province - 'Province of Good Hope' or previously the Cape Colony

slides.title: Colonisation of the Cape, 1600s-1700s
slides.lead: How a Dutch trading company built a settlement at the Cape - and how that settlement changed the lives of the Khoikhoi, the San and thousands of enslaved people.

media.alt: A sailing ship on the sea near Table Mountain, with a small fort on the shore.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#dfeaf0'/><rect y='120' width='320' height='60' fill='#3f6b7a'/><path d='M0 120 L70 78 L120 96 L180 70 L250 92 L320 74 L320 120 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='40' y='104' width='36' height='16' fill='#8a6a3a'/><g><rect x='210' y='70' width='4' height='54' fill='#4a3620'/><path d='M214 74 L252 96 L214 100 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M210 74 L172 96 L210 100 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='188' y='110' width='48' height='14' fill='#5c4326'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what they think a 'colony' is and why European ships were sailing past the Cape in the 1600s. Set up the two big ideas of this lesson: (1) the Dutch came to trade, not at first to conquer, but their settlement grew and took land; (2) the settlement was built on the labour of enslaved people and led to the dispossession of the Khoikhoi and San. Explain 'colonisation' simply - when people from one country settle in and take control of another people's land.

slides.title: The first people of the Cape
slides.lead: The Khoikhoi and the San had lived in southern Africa for thousands of years before any ship arrived.

blocks.text: Two groups lived at the Cape long before the Dutch. The **San** were hunter-gatherers who moved with the seasons, hunting with poisoned arrows and gathering wild plants. The **Khoikhoi** were herders (pastoralists) who kept large herds of cattle and sheep and measured their wealth in livestock.

items: The name 'Khoikhoi' is often said to mean 'men among men' or 'people people'.
The Khoikhoi moved their herds to fresh grazing land near the coast and rivers.
Together the two groups are sometimes called the Khoisan, as they shared languages with click sounds.

media.alt: Cattle grazing on grassland with two herders standing nearby.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#eef3e6'/><rect y='108' width='220' height='52' fill='#8aa05a'/><g fill='#6b4a2a'><ellipse cx='70' cy='96' rx='22' ry='13'/><rect x='52' y='96' width='6' height='16'/><rect x='84' y='96' width='6' height='16'/><ellipse cx='140' cy='104' rx='18' ry='11'/><rect x='126' y='104' width='5' height='14'/><rect x='151' y='104' width='5' height='14'/></g><g fill='#3f2d1a'><circle cx='30' cy='70' r='7'/><rect x='27' y='78' width='6' height='24'/><circle cx='190' cy='66' r='7'/><rect x='187' y='74' width='6' height='26'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Emphasise that the Cape was not empty land - it was home to settled communities with their own economy, languages and ways of life. This matters for everything that follows: when the Dutch took grazing land, they were taking it from people who depended on it. Correct the old myth that the land was 'unused'. Note that San and Khoikhoi lifeways overlapped and people sometimes moved between them.

slides.title: The VOC: a trading company
slides.lead: The Dutch East India Company wanted spices from Asia - and a safe place to stop on the long sea route.

blocks.text: The **Dutch East India Company** (in Dutch, the *Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie*, or **VOC**) was founded in **1602**. It was one of the richest companies in the world and traded in spices like pepper, cinnamon and nutmeg between Europe and Asia.

items: The sea voyage from the Netherlands to Asia took many months.
Sailors often became sick and died from a disease called scurvy, caused by a poor diet.
The VOC needed a 'halfway station' where ships could take on fresh water, vegetables and meat.

media.alt: A world map arrow curving from Europe around the tip of Africa toward Asia, with a star at the Cape.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#dfeaf0'/><path d='M40 30 Q60 90 120 130 Q180 100 210 40' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><circle cx='40' cy='30' r='6' fill='#3f6b45'/><circle cx='210' cy='40' r='6' fill='#3f6b45'/><path d='M120 122 l6 12 l6 -12 l-6 4 Z' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M112 130 l8 -3 l8 3 l-8 12 Z' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain that the VOC was not a government at first but a private trading company - yet it was so powerful it could raise armies, build forts and make treaties. Link the need for a station to the earlier voyages of the Portuguese (Dias, da Gama) who had already rounded the Cape. Make the human reason concrete: scurvy killed many sailors, so fresh food at the Cape saved lives and money.

slides.title: Jan van Riebeeck arrives
slides.lead: The VOC sent Jan van Riebeeck to build a refreshment station at Table Bay.

blocks.text: On **6 April 1652**, **Jan van Riebeeck** landed at Table Bay with a small group of Company employees. Their job was to build a fort and grow food to supply passing ships. At the start, the Cape was meant to be only a supply station, **not a colony**.

items: The Dutch built a mud-and-timber fort for protection.
They planted the Company's Garden to grow fresh vegetables and fruit.
Van Riebeeck was ordered to trade peacefully with the Khoikhoi for cattle and sheep.

media.alt: A square fort with four corner points and a flag, beside a vegetable garden.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#eef3e6'/><rect y='118' width='220' height='42' fill='#8aa05a'/><g fill='#b08a56'><path d='M40 60 h70 v58 h-70 Z'/><path d='M40 60 l-12 -12 v12 Z'/><path d='M110 60 l12 -12 v12 Z'/><path d='M40 118 l-12 12 h12 Z'/><path d='M110 118 l12 12 h-12 Z'/></g><rect x='72' y='34' width='3' height='26' fill='#4a3620'/><path d='M75 34 l16 6 l-16 6 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#3f6b45'><rect x='140' y='96' width='10' height='22'/><rect x='156' y='90' width='10' height='28'/><rect x='172' y='98' width='10' height='20'/><rect x='188' y='92' width='10' height='26'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Stress the word 'refreshment station' - the Dutch did not plan to build a big colony. This is important for showing change over time: what began small grew into a settlement that took over the whole region. Mention that van Riebeeck kept a detailed diary, which is a key primary source historians still use. Ask learners to predict what might cause conflict once the Dutch needed more and more land and food.

slides.title: From station to settlement
slides.lead: Within a few years the small station began spreading onto Khoikhoi land.

blocks.text: In **1657** the VOC allowed some of its employees to become **free burghers** - free citizens who left the Company to farm their own land near the fort. To feed the growing settlement they needed more land and more workers, so the settlement pushed outward from Table Bay.

items: Free burghers were given farms on land the Khoikhoi used for grazing.
In 1688 French Huguenot refugees arrived and helped start wine farming.
As farms spread, the settlers needed a large, cheap workforce.

media.alt: Neat rows of farm crops with a gabled Cape farmhouse behind them.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#eef3e6'/><rect y='104' width='220' height='56' fill='#9a7d54'/><g stroke='#3f6b45' stroke-width='4'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='40' y2='150'/><line x1='55' y1='120' x2='75' y2='150'/><line x1='90' y1='120' x2='110' y2='150'/></g><rect x='138' y='70' width='60' height='40' fill='#f4efe6' stroke='#8a6a3a' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M138 70 q30 -22 60 0 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='160' y='86' width='16' height='24' fill='#6b4a2a'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This slide is the hinge of the lesson: the shift from a supply station to a spreading settler colony. Explain 'free burgher' plainly - workers who became independent farmers. Two forces now drive colonisation: hunger for land (taken from the Khoikhoi) and hunger for labour (which leads to slavery, the next slides). The 1688 Huguenots are a good example of the settler population growing and mixing.

slides.title: The Dutch and the Khoikhoi
slides.lead: As settlers took grazing land, trade turned into war and the Khoikhoi lost their independence.

blocks.text: At first the Khoikhoi traded cattle to the Dutch. But as farms spread onto their pastures, the Khoikhoi lost grazing land and animals. This led to the **first Khoikhoi-Dutch War (1659-1660)** and a **second war (1673-1677)**. The Dutch, with guns and horses, gradually took control.

items: Khoikhoi leaders like Doman led resistance against the loss of their land.
Autshumato (called 'Harry' by the Dutch) acted as a trader and translator.
Krotoa, a young Khoikhoi woman, worked as an interpreter in van Riebeeck's household.

media.alt: A fence line dividing grazing land, with cattle on one side and a settler house on the other.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#eef3e6'/><rect y='110' width='220' height='50' fill='#8aa05a'/><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='4'><line x1='108' y1='60' x2='108' y2='150'/><line x1='90' y1='84' x2='126' y2='84'/><line x1='90' y1='104' x2='126' y2='104'/></g><g fill='#6b4a2a'><ellipse cx='50' cy='96' rx='18' ry='11'/><rect x='36' y='96' width='5' height='14'/><rect x='60' y='96' width='5' height='14'/></g><rect x='150' y='74' width='44' height='36' fill='#f4efe6' stroke='#8a6a3a' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M150 74 q22 -16 44 0 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this as the heart of colonial dispossession, at a Grade 7 level. Trade was unequal from the start, and once land was fenced for farms the Khoikhoi could not keep their herds moving. Name the people so history is not faceless: Doman (resistance), Autshumato (trade/translation), Krotoa/Eva (interpreter caught between two worlds). [SME] Please review the framing of Krotoa - her life story is complex and sometimes contested; confirm the classroom-appropriate wording before production.

slides.title: Why the VOC brought enslaved people
slides.lead: The Company would not let the settlers enslave the Khoikhoi, so it imported enslaved people from across the Indian Ocean.

blocks.text: The settlers needed workers for their farms and homes, but the VOC forbade the enslavement of the local Khoikhoi. Instead the Company **imported enslaved people** by ship. The first large groups arrived in **1658** on the ships *Amersfoort* and *Hasselt*, and the Cape slowly became a **slave society**.

items: Enslaved people did farm work, building, cooking and household labour.
Owning enslaved people became normal for many settler families.
By the 1700s there were often more enslaved people at the Cape than free settlers.

media.alt: A pair of broken chain links against a plain background, symbolising enslavement.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#e9e2d4'/><g fill='none' stroke='#5c5142' stroke-width='10'><ellipse cx='84' cy='80' rx='24' ry='34'/><ellipse cx='140' cy='80' rx='24' ry='34'/></g><rect x='104' y='72' width='16' height='16' fill='#e9e2d4'/></svg>

slides.notes: Introduce slavery carefully and clearly. Make the logic plain: the Company protected the Khoikhoi from enslavement (partly to keep the cattle trade), but it had no problem shipping in enslaved people from elsewhere. Stress that enslaved people were human beings treated as property against their will - not 'servants'. The striking fact that the enslaved sometimes outnumbered free settlers shows how central slavery was to the whole colony. [SME] Confirm the 1658 Amersfoort/Hasselt detail is at the right depth for Grade 7.

slides.title: Where enslaved people came from
slides.lead: People were captured and shipped to the Cape from many lands around the Indian Ocean.

blocks.text: Enslaved people were brought to the Cape from **East Africa, Madagascar, India and the islands of what is now Indonesia**. Torn from their homes and families, they arrived speaking many different languages. In Cape Town, Company-owned enslaved people were held in the **Slave Lodge**.

items: Enslaved people brought their own skills, foods, music and beliefs.
Many were Muslim, and Islam took root at the Cape through them.
Their cultures helped shape the language and food we now call Cape Malay.

media.alt: Arrows pointing from four directions toward a single point marked at the Cape.

slides.notes: Use a wall map if you have one, tracing the Indian Ocean routes. Emphasise the human cost: people were separated from family and homeland and forced to build lives among strangers. Then highlight resilience and legacy - enslaved communities helped create Cape Malay cooking, the Afrikaans language and a lasting Muslim community. Mention the Slave Lodge in Cape Town, which learners can still visit as a museum today.

slides.title: Harsh lives, strong spirits
slides.lead: Enslaved people had almost no rights, yet they found ways to resist and to keep their humanity.

blocks.text: Enslaved people could be bought and sold, were forced to work long hours, and were punished harshly. Strict laws controlled where they could go and what they could do. Yet even under these cruel conditions, people **resisted** - by running away, working slowly, keeping their beliefs, or in some cases rising up.

items: Enslaved people were not allowed to own property or move freely.
Families could be broken apart when members were sold to different owners.
Holding on to language, faith and music was itself a form of resistance.

media.alt: A small barred window with a bright sunrise showing through it.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#3a3a44'/><rect x='64' y='36' width='92' height='88' rx='4' fill='#e8a13a'/><circle cx='110' cy='96' r='24' fill='#f6d27a'/><g stroke='#3a3a44' stroke-width='7'><line x1='90' y1='36' x2='90' y2='124'/><line x1='110' y1='36' x2='110' y2='124'/><line x1='130' y1='36' x2='130' y2='124'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This slide asks learners to think about both cruelty and courage. Keep the tone honest but not graphic - name the injustice clearly while focusing on the dignity and resistance of enslaved people, so the lesson does not reduce them only to victims. Good discussion prompt: why might keeping your language or religion count as an act of resistance? [SME] Please review the level of detail on punishment and any named uprising you wish to add for Grade 7 sensitivity.

slides.title: Colonial expansion inland
slides.lead: During the 1700s settler farmers pushed far beyond Cape Town, taking more and more land.

blocks.text: By the 1700s, cattle farmers called **trekboers** loaded their families onto ox-wagons and moved deep into the interior in search of grazing land. As the colony's **frontier** pushed north and east, it kept taking land from the Khoikhoi and San, who resisted but were pushed off their land or forced to work for the settlers.

items: Trekboers lived a semi-nomadic life far from the Company's control.
The moving frontier brought new conflicts over land, cattle and water.
Many Khoikhoi and San were killed, displaced, or made to labour on settler farms.

media.alt: An ox-wagon pulled by oxen moving across open country toward distant hills.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#eef3e6'/><path d='M0 118 L80 100 L150 112 L240 96 L240 118 Z' fill='#b0a06a'/><rect y='118' width='240' height='42' fill='#9a7d54'/><g fill='#6b4a2a'><ellipse cx='60' cy='96' rx='14' ry='9'/><rect x='48' y='96' width='4' height='12'/><rect x='68' y='96' width='4' height='12'/><ellipse cx='92' cy='96' rx='14' ry='9'/><rect x='80' y='96' width='4' height='12'/><rect x='100' y='96' width='4' height='12'/></g><path d='M120 80 q28 -16 56 0 v22 h-56 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='none' stroke='#4a3620' stroke-width='3'><circle cx='138' cy='108' r='9'/><circle cx='166' cy='108' r='9'/></g><line x1='104' y1='96' x2='128' y2='96' stroke='#4a3620' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain 'frontier' as the always-moving edge of the colony. The trekboers show how colonisation kept spreading long after 1652, far from Company control. Keep the consequences clear: this expansion meant continued dispossession, violence and forced labour for Khoikhoi and San communities. This links forward to the frontier wars learners will meet in later grades.

slides.title: The smallpox epidemic
slides.lead: A disease carried by ship helped shatter Khoikhoi society at the Cape.

blocks.text: In **1713** a **smallpox** epidemic swept through the Cape. The Khoikhoi had never been exposed to this European disease and had no resistance to it, so huge numbers died. Together with the loss of their land and cattle, the epidemic broke apart many Khoikhoi communities forever.

items: Smallpox is thought to have arrived on the laundry of a passing ship.
Whole Khoikhoi clans lost most of their members.
Survivors often had little choice but to work as labourers for the settlers.

media.alt: A ship at anchor with faint dotted lines spreading from it toward the shore.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#dfeaf0'/><rect y='104' width='240' height='56' fill='#3f6b7a'/><g><rect x='60' y='60' width='4' height='44' fill='#4a3620'/><path d='M64 64 L98 84 L64 90 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M60 64 L26 84 L60 90 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='40' y='94' width='44' height='12' fill='#5c4326'/></g><g fill='#8a1f1f'><circle cx='120' cy='96' r='3'/><circle cx='150' cy='104' r='3'/><circle cx='178' cy='94' r='3'/><circle cx='202' cy='106' r='3'/><circle cx='138' cy='118' r='3'/><circle cx='190' cy='120' r='3'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain why 'virgin soil' epidemics were so deadly: the Khoikhoi had no built-up immunity to smallpox. Connect the three blows that broke Khoikhoi independence - loss of land, loss of cattle, and now disease. Be careful not to suggest disease was the only cause; it worked together with colonisation and dispossession. [SME] Verify the 1713 date and the 'ship's laundry' explanation against SAHO before production.

slides.title: Key dates, 1602-1713

blocks.alt: A timeline running from the founding of the VOC in 1602 to the smallpox epidemic of 1713.

events.label: The VOC (Dutch East India Company) is founded
Jan van Riebeeck lands at Table Bay
First free burghers get their own farms
First large groups of enslaved people arrive

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate the sequence before the review. Ask learners to spot the pattern: the Dutch arrive to trade, the settlement grows, land is taken, enslaved people are brought in, and the Khoikhoi lose their independence. Have learners copy the timeline into their books and add one sentence explaining why each date matters.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: A trading stop grew into a colony built on stolen land and forced labour - changing the Cape forever.

blocks.text: The Cape began as a VOC **refreshment station** in 1652, but it grew into a settler colony. Colonisation meant the **dispossession** of the Khoikhoi and San, and a society built on the **slavery** of people brought from across the Indian Ocean. These changes still shape South Africa's land, languages and people today.

blocks.question: Why did the VOC want a station at the Cape, and why did it grow into a colony?
Explain two ways the Khoikhoi lost their land and independence in the 1600s and 1700s.
Where were enslaved people brought from, and how did they help shape life at the Cape?

slides.notes: Close by returning to the big ideas: colonisation was about land and labour. Use the three questions as a written task or class discussion; the mark allocations follow CAPS source-based and paragraph-writing styles. Remind learners that this history connects to the land, languages (like Afrikaans) and communities (like the Cape Muslim community) they know today, and sets up later topics on the British at the Cape and the frontier wars.
